label: wealth buys regulatory influence
note: Concentrated wealth funds lobbying, campaign contributions, and revolving door hiring that systematically captures regulatory agencies.

label: wealth enables political extraction
note: Capital concentration funds the lobbying and political donations needed to secure monopoly protections, tax preferences, and other rents.

label: capital returns exceed wage growth
note: Piketty's r > g: returns to capital exceed economic growth, systematically concentrating income among asset owners.

label: compound returns and political capture
note: Accumulated wealth generates returns that compound while simultaneously purchasing political protection against redistribution.

label: weak antitrust enforcement
note: Chicago School capture of antitrust agencies redefined predatory pricing as implausible, allowing monopolization to proceed unchecked.

label: banks wrote their own oversight rules
note: Financial industry regulatory capture (Gramm-Leach-Bliley, SEC leverage exemptions, CFTC derivatives exclusion) removed the oversight that could have prevented the 2008 collapse.

label: asset returns accrue to owners
note: As economic activity shifted to financial extraction, gains flowed to asset owners rather than workers, accelerating concentration.

label: complex instruments built systemic fragility
note: Financialization created mortgage-backed securities, CDOs, and credit default swap chains that amplified the subprime shock into a global crisis.

label: firms outsource labor risk
note: Shareholder primacy and financialization pressure caused firms to shed employment risk through contracting, temp work, and the gig model.

label: austerity as core neoliberal policy
note: Austerity is the fiscal expression of neoliberal theory: government deficit spending is treated as crowding out private investment.

label: deregulation enabled financial expansion
note: Capital account liberalization, Glass-Steagall repeal, and derivatives deregulation created conditions for the financial sector's dominance.

label: union busting as policy
note: Reagan's PATCO firing, Taft-Hartley, right-to-work laws, and NLRB capture all reflect neoliberal ideology's hostility to collective bargaining.

label: capital mobility without labor mobility
note: Free trade agreements without labor or environmental standards enable jurisdictions to compete by lowering protections.

label: structural adjustment as neoliberal export
note: The Washington Consensus encoded neoliberal doctrine as IMF/World Bank loan conditionality, spreading it to the Global South.

label: tax policy favoring capital
note: Reagan and subsequent tax cuts lowered top marginal rates, capital gains taxes, and estate taxes, accelerating concentration.

label: intellectual infrastructure for neoliberalism
note: Friedman's monetarism, Stigler's regulatory capture theory, and Lucas's rational expectations provided neoliberalism its academic credibility.

label: Chicago Boys implemented shock programs
note: Chicago-trained economists in Chile, Bolivia, and Russia implemented neoliberal programs under crisis cover.

label: ideology justifies light-touch regulation
note: Greenspan's market fundamentalism led him to oppose derivatives regulation, trusting firms to self-regulate.

label: share price as sole corporate metric
note: Friedman's shareholder doctrine enabled buybacks, short-termism, and financial engineering replacing productive investment.

label: labor is a cost to minimize
note: When returns to shareholders are the only metric, wages are pressure to reduce rather than a social obligation.

label: crisis created mandate for intervention
note: The Great Depression discredited laissez-faire and created the political mandate for FDR's regulatory and social programs.

label: market failure demanded new theory
note: Classical economics could not explain mass unemployment in equilibrium; Keynes's General Theory emerged from the Depression crisis.

label: unregulated banking created systemic fragility
note: Absence of deposit insurance, banking regulation, and automatic stabilizers allowed the 1929 crash to cascade into a decade-long depression.

label: enclosure as primitive accumulation
note: The enclosure movement is Marx's archetypal case of primitive accumulation: commons to private property by legal fiat.

label: landless proletariat created wage labor
note: Dispossessed peasants became the industrial workforce, making wage labor markets possible.

label: monopoly capitalism concentrates wealth
note: Standard Oil, US Steel, and railroad trusts produced the first great wealth concentration under industrial capitalism.

label: industrial violence against unions
note: Carnegie (Homestead 1892), Rockefeller (Ludlow 1914), and Pullman all used private armies and state militias against labor organizing.

label: conditionality perpetuates indebtedness
note: SAP conditions that suppress growth and privatize revenue-generating assets prevent countries from repaying, requiring further loans and conditions.

label: labor cost competition enabled
note: Structural adjustment requirements for open capital accounts and labor market flexibility enable capital to flee toward lowest-standard countries.

label: bailouts privatized gains, socialized losses
note: Bank bailouts preserved the wealth of financial asset holders while homeowners lost equity, accelerating the wealth gap.

label: floating rates enabled currency speculation
note: Ending gold convertibility created floating exchange rates and enabled the capital account liberalization that powered financialization.

label: crisis exploitation mechanism
note: Both use economic crisis or coercion to impose neoliberal reforms: SAPs require economic distress; shock doctrine exploits manufactured or natural crises.

label: wage share decline creates precarity
note: Declining labor share of income forces more workers into non-standard employment to maintain consumption.

label: resentment redirected from class to culture
note: Increasing inequality produces anxiety and resentment that trickle-down propaganda and scapegoating redirect toward racial/cultural targets.

label: regulatory capture enables market dominance
note: Rent-seeking via regulatory capture produces monopoly protections: exclusive licenses, favorable antitrust interpretation, and incumbent advantages.

label: monopoly profits accrue to owners
note: Monopoly pricing extracts above-competitive returns that flow to shareholders, accelerating concentration.

label: stagnant wages widen the gap
note: When productivity gains are not shared with workers, the income gap between capital and labor grows.

label: social spending cuts used as investment signal
note: Countries compete to cut social spending as a signal to bond markets and foreign investors.

label: unions removed from wage-setting
note: Union decline directly explains the disconnection of productivity and wage growth: collective bargaining was the mechanism of wage transmission.

label: workers vote for tax cuts for the wealthy
note: False consciousness about class position enables political support for policies (supply-side tax cuts) that harm working-class material interests.

label: 1944 agreement created system that collapsed in 1971
note: The Bretton Woods Agreement established the gold-dollar peg that Nixon abandoned in 1971, triggering financialization.

label: created the IMF that later imposed structural adjustment
note: The IMF created at Bretton Woods later became the instrument for imposing structural adjustment conditions on developing nations.

label: Fed's contractionary policy worsened the Depression
note: The Federal Reserve tightened the money supply in 1929–32, transforming a recession into the Great Depression — a catastrophic policy error.

label: repeal enabled the risky banking that crashed in 2008
note: Glass-Steagall repeal allowed banks to become 'too big to fail' and to create the mortgage-backed securities that triggered the 2008 crisis.

label: bank merger with investment firms accelerated financialization
note: Allowing commercial banks to engage in investment banking accelerated the shift from productive to financial capitalism.

label: bubble collapse triggered global financial crisis
note: The collapse of the U.S. housing bubble in 2007–08 triggered the global financial crisis through interconnected mortgage securities.

label: crash disproportionately destroyed minority wealth
note: Black and Hispanic homeowners were disproportionately targeted by predatory lending and suffered greater wealth loss in the crash.

label: offshoring threat suppressed wages in manufacturing
note: The credible threat to move production to Mexico gave U.S. employers leverage to suppress wages and resist unions.

label: enabled race to the bottom in labor and environmental standards
note: NAFTA created regulatory competition between U.S., Mexico, and Canada that pressured all three to lower labor and environmental standards.

label: both imposed neoliberal trade logic globally
note: NAFTA and the WTO embodied the same Washington Consensus free-trade ideology applied at different scales.

label: WTO rules prevented regulation of corporate behavior
note: WTO dispute mechanisms allowed corporations to challenge national regulations as 'barriers to trade', enabling regulatory race to the bottom.

label: QE inflated asset prices benefiting only asset owners
note: QE's primary mechanism (raising asset prices) disproportionately benefited the wealthy who own most financial assets, worsening inequality.

label: QE was the Fed's response to 2008 collapse
note: Quantitative easing was deployed in response to the 2008 financial crisis when conventional monetary policy hit the zero lower bound.

label: gig work is precariat labor at scale
note: The gig economy is the primary contemporary mechanism producing the precariat — workers with no employment security, benefits, or collective power.

label: independent contractor classification avoids minimum wage
note: By classifying workers as contractors, gig companies avoid minimum wage obligations and push wage risk onto workers.

label: tax evasion lets the wealthy accumulate without redistribution
note: Offshore tax evasion removes trillions from public taxation, concentrating wealth while cutting government capacity to address inequality.

label: lobbying kept tax havens legal despite public cost
note: The maintenance of offshore tax havens despite massive public harm is a textbook example of regulatory capture by financial interests.

label: debt burden suppresses wealth accumulation for young people
note: Student debt delays homeownership, retirement saving, and family formation, locking borrowers into perpetual income disadvantage.

label: austerity-driven state disinvestment in universities caused the crisis
note: The student debt crisis was primarily caused by state austerity policies cutting public university funding, shifting costs to students.

label: neoliberal deregulation ideology drove repeal
note: The repeal was a product of the neoliberal belief in market self-regulation that dominated both parties in the 1990s.

label: NAFTA was the Clinton administration's neoliberal trade policy
note: NAFTA embodied the Washington Consensus neoliberal belief that free trade produces prosperity regardless of starting conditions.

label: neoliberal austerity defunded public universities
note: The neoliberal shift to viewing education as a private investment rather than public good drove the disinvestment that created the student debt crisis.

label: deregulation and labor market 'flexibility' enabled gig model
note: The gig economy depends on the deregulated labor markets and weakened worker protections that neoliberalism produced.

label: financial engineering turned mortgages into speculative vehicles
note: Financialization transformed stable mortgages into complex securities that enabled and amplified the housing bubble.

label: both extend economic resources to previously excluded populations
note: Social democracy (through universal social programs) and microfinance (through small business credit) both work to extend economic resources to those excluded by market failure — social democracy through the state, microfinance through market mechanisms. They represent the two poles of the debate about whether poverty is best addressed through universal public provision or individual market access.

label: East Asian development drove the majority of the global poverty reduction since 1990
note: China alone accounts for approximately 800 million people lifted from extreme poverty since 1978, representing roughly two-thirds of global poverty reduction. South Korea, Taiwan, and Southeast Asian economies account for hundreds of millions more. Without East Asian development, global poverty would have remained near its 1990 level.

label: both are alternative economic institutions that bypass conventional capital markets
note: Cooperatives and microfinance both operate on the insight that conventional capital markets systematically exclude populations that are economically productive — workers who can't raise equity, poor entrepreneurs who can't post collateral. Both create alternative institutional forms that extend productive economic participation to excluded groups.

label: fair trade provides price floor protection for smallholder farmers
note: Fair trade certification provides smallholder farmers — coffee, cocoa, banana growers — a price floor above commodity market prices and guaranteed market access, partially insulating them from the commodity price volatility that has been a major driver of agricultural poverty. Impact studies show meaningful income improvements in certified cooperatives, contributing to poverty reduction at the margin.

label: dollar hegemony created architecture for financial globalization
note: Bretton Woods established the dollar reserve system — and its 1971 collapse produced floating exchange rates that incentivized financial speculation and currency trading. The post-Bretton-Woods era of floating currencies and capital mobility drove financialization.

label: WTO intellectual property rules enabled global corporate monopolization
note: WTO's TRIPS agreement (Trade-Related Intellectual Property) extended US patent and copyright standards globally — enabling pharmaceutical and technology companies to maintain global monopoly pricing on drugs and software, concentrating market power at global scale.

label: contractor classification prevents workers from organizing unions
note: By classifying gig workers as independent contractors rather than employees, platforms remove them from the legal framework that enables collective bargaining — systematically preventing the union organizing that might otherwise emerge from shared working conditions.

label: QE created a decade of asset price inflation that enriched the already-wealthy
note: Ten years of near-zero interest rates drove capital into equities, real estate, and alternative assets — producing sustained asset price inflation. Those who owned assets got dramatically wealthier; those who relied on wages or savings did not.

label: tax avoidance depletes revenues, creating the fiscal gaps that austerity fills
note: Corporate and elite tax avoidance through offshore havens depletes government revenues — creating fiscal gaps that politicians use to justify cutting public services. Tax avoidance creates the fiscal crisis that becomes the justification for austerity.
